Re:
Fern Polrrrcel PRAcrrces C oMMrssroN
.128 J Street r Suite 620 r Sacramento. CA 9-581;l-2329
\916) 322-5660 o Fax t916) 322-0886
April 28,2009
Stacey Simon
County of Mono
Office of the County Counsel
Post Office Box 2415
Mammoth Lakes, CA93546
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.I-09-076
Dear Ms. Simon:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of each memberl of the Mono
County Fisheries Commission for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").2 Because you seek general information and have not
provided information on any specific governmental decision, we offer informal assistance.3
Nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that has already taken
place. In addition, this letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices
Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact. (ln re Oglesby (1975) I
FPPC Ops.7l.)
QUESTTONS
Does John Webb, Ray Robles, Jeremy Ross, Jim King, or Kevin Peterson have a
conflict of interest that would prevent them from participating in any of the following
activities?
I The members are: John Webb, Ray Robles, Jeremy Ross, Jim King, and Kevin Peterson.
I The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All
statutory references are to the Government Code. unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair
Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18997 of Tirle 2 of the California
Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.
I Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4l Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).


File No. I-09-076
Page No. 2
1. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the stocking of
fish in Little Virginia Lake or the other Virginia Lakes (as to Commissioner Webb), in
the June Lakes (as to Jeremy Ross), in Rock Creek Lake or Rock Creek (as to Jim
KnighQ or in any of the lakes in Mono County (as to Kevin Peterson and Ray Robles).
2. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the stocking of
fish in lakes other than Little Virginia Lake or the othei Virginia Lakes (a-s to John
Webb), other than the June Lakes (as to Jeremy Ross), or other than Rock Creek Lake or
Rock Creek (as to Jim King).
3. Making recorlmendations to the board of supervisors regarding the
expenditure of county funds to enhance the amount of fish raised o, Con*ay Ranch.
4. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the
expenditure of county funds for the general management and op"raiio, oithe Conway
Ranch fish-rearing facility.
, 
5' Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding ways in which
the county could raise revenue (such as fundraisers, taies, grants, etc.; toinhance the fish
rearing and stocking in Mono Lake waters.
6. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding actions the
County could take to enhance the amount of fish raisedby DFG or privatl hatcheries and
stocked in Mono County waters.
7. Making recorlmendations to the board of supervisors regarding actions the
county could take to increase fishing related tourism in Mono county.
CONCLUSION
Any of the commissioners would have a conflict of interest participating in any of
the governmental decisions enumerated above if the governmental decision *o-uta have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the his economic interests as
discussed below.
FACTS
In January 2004 yott requested informal written advice from the Commission
regarding potential conflicts of interest involving two appointed members of the Mono
County Fisheries Commission (the "Fisheries Commission") given their ownership of
fishing-related businesses adjacent to highly-visited fishing areas within Mono County
(the "County"). (Simorc Advice Letter, No. I-04-013.) You now seek further advice on
the same questions posed in 2004 in light of recent changes in facts and circumstances
that you believe may alter the prior analysis.


File No. l-09-076
Page No. 3
Specifically, in our response to your prior request, we determined that neither of
the two commissioners, upon who's behalf the request was made, had a conflict of
interest in participating in activities of the Fisheries Commission because it was advisory
only and, as such, the two commissioners were not "public officials" within the meaning
of the Act.
However, since the time that advice was given, the Mono county Board of
Supervisors (the "Board") has allocated $25,000 of general fund money to the Fisheries
Commission for its expenditure in support of recreational fishing in Mono County. [n
other words, the Fisheries Commission has been given decision-making authority with
respect to the expenditure of $25,000. This one-time allocation resulted from a request
made by the Fish Commission to the Board at its 2008-2009 fiscal year budget hearing.
The Fisheries Commission's original request was for $40,000.
Additionally, since 2004, several new members have been appointed to the
Fisheries Commission, and John Frederickson - who was one of the subjects of the prior
request, has resigned.
BACKGROUND: Mono County owns and operates a fish-rearing facility iocated
at Conway Ranch. Mono County uses fish raised at Conway Ranch to supplement fish
stocking in Mono County waters otherwise carried out by the California Oepartment of
Fish and Game (DFG), private parties, and the County itself (the latter two using fish
purchased from private hatcheries).
The Fisheries Commission was created by a resolution of the Board for the
purpose of advising the Board with respect to Conway Ranch and fish rearing and
stocking within the County generally. The duties of the Fisheries Commission include
making recommendations regarding where fish raised on Conway Ranch should be
stocked, making recommendations regarding expenditure of County funds to purchase
fish raised at other facilities for stocking in Mono County, making.."o.n-.ndations
regarding methods for enhancing the amount of fish raised and stocked by private entities
and/or DFG in Mono County, and making recommendations regarding ttre entrancement
of fishing-related tourism in Mono County. Additionally, the Fisheriei Commission is
charged with drafting a fisheries management plan for the County, which has not yet
been completed.
The Fisheries Commission was created by Resolution of the Board of Supervisors
for the purpose of advising the board with respect to the Conway Ranch and fistt-rearing
and stocking within the County generally. The duties of the Fisheries Commission
includes: making recommendations regarding where fish raised on Conway Ranch should
be stocked; making recommendations regarding the expenditure of county funds to
purchase fish raised at other facilities for stocking in Mono County; making
recommendations regarding methods for enhancing the amount of fish raised and stocked
by private entities and/or DFG in Mono County; and making recommendations regarding
the enhancement of fishing-related tourism in Mono County. Additionally, the Fisheries


File No. I-09-076
Page No.4
Commission is charged with drafting a fisheries management plan for the County, which
has not yet been completed.
The Fisheries Commission's recommendations to the Board are advisory only
although, in the four years since it was created, the Board has declined to follow the
Fisheries Commission's advice in one instance only - when it did not approve its request
that $40,000 be placed in the Fisheries Commission's budget to be allocated in support of
recreational fish enhancement. As mentioned above, the Board instead allocated
$25,000, which has made the Fisheries Commission a decision maker as to the
expenditure of those funds.
Flcrs PornNrr.lr,r,y CREATTNG A CoNrurcr
l. One of the members of the Fisheries Commission, John Webb, owns a small
resort located adjacent to Little Virginia Lake, a popular fishing destination in Mono
County. The resort consists of 19 individual cabins, a small store at which food, beer,
souvenirs, sundries, and fishing, hiking, and camping supplies are sold, a small
restaurant, and a boat rental operation. Many of the resort guests have come to the area
to fish, and the lake has historically been stocked with fish by both DFG and the County,
including fish raised by the County at Conway Ranch. The resort offers the only lodging,
food, and supplies within several miles of Virginia Lake and the several other lakes
within that watershed (the "other Virginia Lakes").
2. Another member, Jim King, owns Rock Creek Resort adjacent to Rock Creek
Lake and Rock Creek. The resort consists of 11 rental cabins, a general store, caf6, and
boat rentals on Rock Creek. The store carries fishing-related supplies such as tackle and
bait, a well as camping and other supplies. Many of the resort's customers have come to
the area to fish. While the lake and resort themselves are actually located in tnyo County
- Mono's neighbor to the south - Mono County does stock (and supports the stocking
by others of) Rock Creek Lake and Rock Creek, as the County receives revenue from ihe
campgrounds in the area, which are frequented by fishermen visiting the lake and creek.
The resort is I quarter-mile from the Mono County line.
3. A third membei, Jeremy Ross, is employed by a sporting goods shop in June
Lake, which is the commercial hub for visitors recreating at Gull, Silver, June, and Grant
Lakes (collectively "the June Lakes") in Mono County. Each of the June Lakes is a
popular fishing spot and is stocked with fish by the County and the DFG, including with
fish raised at Conway Ranch. The sporting goods shop primarily sells fishing bait and
tackle, as do other shops in June Lake.
4. A fourth member, Ray Robles, owns a for-profit fishing guide business in
which he takes members of the public on fishing trips within the County, primarily on
Bridgeport Reservoir.


File No. t-09-076
Page No. 5
5. A fifth member, Kevin Peterson owns a fishing guide business in which he
takes members of the public on fishing expeditions throughout the state (including in
Mono County waters) and to locations outside the country such as Central America.
Mr. Peterson has the ability to and does guide his customers to any water within the
County in which the customers wish to fish.
AI\ALYSIS
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused bytheir own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Section
87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otirerwise
using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision iriwhich the official
has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700.) The geneial rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official mat<es-, participates
in making, or otherwise uses his or her official positionio influence a governmental
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect o, ore or more of his
or her financial interests.
Srnps 1: Anr rHE MEMBERS oF TlrE FTsHERTES CoMMrssroN punr,rc oFFrcrALs?
Section 82048 states that "public official" means "every member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.;' Regulation lg70l(a)
provides that for purposes of section 52048 defining public official,ihe following
definitions apply:
"(l) 'Member' shall include, but not be rimited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authortty. A committee, board or commission possesses decision -utirg
authority whenever:
(A) 
It may make a final govemmental decision;
(B) 
It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive po*er to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not 6e
overridden; or
(c) 
It makes substantive recommendations that are, and over
an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by anothei public official
or govemmental agency." (Emphasis added.)


File No. I-09-076
Page No.6
ln our previous letter (Simon Advice Letter, No. I-04-013), we advised that
"[s]ince the individuals are not public officials at this time, they do not have a conflict
of interest within the meaning of the Act. (Regulation 18700(bxl).) However, . . . if
there is a history or track record of "rubber stamping" an advisory body's
recommendations, the advisory body will be considered to have decision-making
authority. (See, e.g., Andriese Advice Letter, No. 4-03-016; Traverso Advice Letter,
No. I-01-124; Baird Advice Letter, No. 4-94-299; czachAdvice Letter, No. A-91-
5o3; woodbury Advice Letter, 4-90-665; Paley Advice Letter, A-90-5g3; Korade
Advice Letter, 4-89-715; Ball Advice Letrer, I-89-671.)"
"we have in the past advised new advisory bodies that they are in fact
solely advisory until a history of recommendations has been established.
(Ball, Traverso, supra.) once there is a history of a particular advisory
body's recommendations being routinely accepted without amendmenl or
modification, the body converts from a solely advisory function to one of
making, or participating in the making of a governmental decision and
must be incorporated into a conflict of interest code. (Traverso, supra.)
Since we have no track record concerning the commission, we are not able
to determine if its members should be included in the commission's
conflict of interest code. Even if, at this time, as you have concluded, the
commission functions solely as "advisory" as opposed to "making," or
"participating in making," govemmental decisions, we suggest that you
monitor the commission's recommendations and the board of supervisors'
actions with regard to these recommendations over the next several
months and amend the commission's conflict of interest code if required.
If this does become the case, and the commission members are included in
the conflict of interest code, you may then contact us again for advice on
any potential conflict of interest issues."
You have now indicated that the Fisheries Commission has been given decision-
making authority with respect to the expenditure of $25,000, which has been allocated to
it by the Board. Additionally, you have indicated that in the four years since it was
created, the Board has declined to follow the Fisheries Commission's advice in one
instance only - when it did not approve its request that $40,000 be placed in the Fisheries
Commission's budget to be allocated in support of recreational fistr-enhancement but
allocated the $25,000 instead. Given these facts, we believe that the Fisheries
Commission "makes substantive recommendations that are, and over an extended period
of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by
another public official or govemmental agency." Accordingly, members of the Fisheries
Commission now meet the definition of "public official.'l


File No. I-09-076
Page No. 7
Srep2: Ans rHE Mrunrns oF THE FrsHrnms Coprurssrox M.lrlNG, PARTICTpATTNG
IN M.lxrxc, oR OTHTRwISE Ustxc TnBm Orrrcllr PosrrtoNs to INrlunNCE A
DnctsroN?
As public officials under the Act, the Fish Commission members may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use their official positions to influence any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic
interests. They will be called upon to consider certain actions, (see above questions).
Therefore, they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise usinjtheir official
positions to influence a governmental decision.
Srrp 3: Do rnn CoprurssroNERs Hlvr A PoTENTTALLv Drsguu,myrNc EcoNoMrc
Ixrnnrsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a materill
'financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Reg. 1g703.1(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any fosition of
managembnt. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 1g703.1(b).)
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87r03(b); Regulation lg7o3.z.)
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to theGcision. (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420
or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87l03ie); neg-utalion
18703.4.)
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. 1Sectiong7l03;
Regulation 18703.5.)
Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietoiship, partnership, fi..,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association.,,


File No. I-09-076
Page No. 8
Economic Interests of Commissioners ldentified:
From the information you have provided, each commissioner has the following
economic interests:
John Webb: Assuming Commissioner Webb's investment in the resort he owns
near Little Virginia Lake is valued at $2,000 or more, he has an economic interest in a
business entity.4 He also has an economic interest in the resort as a business entity if he
holds a position of management in operating the resort. Finally, he has a source of
income economic interest in the resort if his share of the gross receipts equal $500 or
more in the l2-month period before the governmental decision, and if he owns more than
ten percent of the business, he has an economic interest in the customers of the business
if his pro rata share of the income the resort receives from the customer equals $500 or
more in the l2-month period before the governmental decision.
Jim King: commissioner King's ownership of Rock creek Resort would
ordinarily give him an economic interest in a business entity because of his investment
interest. However, because an investment interest does not include a business entity that
does business, plans to do business or has done business within the last two years in the
jurisdiction, he does not have an economic interest in the resort because of his investment
interest. (See Section82034.) Likewise, because the resort is located outside the
jurisdiction and you have not provided any information indicating that the resort is doing
business within the jurisdiction, planning to do business within th-e jurisdiction, or has
done business within the jurisdiction within the last two years, Commissioner King does
not have an economic interest in the resort as a source of income to him.5 (See Section
82030.) Commissioner King will only have an economic interest in the resort if he is
employed by or has a position of management with the resort. (See Section 87103(d);
Regulation 18703. l(b).)
Jeremy Ross: Commissioner Ross is employed by a sporting goods shop in June
Lake, Mono County, near the June Lakes. He therefore has an economic interest in the
sporting good shop as a business entity and as a source of income to him assuming he has
made $500 or more within the 12 months preceding the govemmental decision.
Ra)'Robles: Commissioner Robles owns a for-profit fishing guide business in
which he takes members of the public on fishing trips within the Couity. He has an
economic interest in the business entity as an employee of the business iand if he has an
investment interest of $2,000 or more) as well as a source of income economic interest in
a For each of the commissioners listed below whom also have an ownership interest in a business
entity, for purposes of our analysis we will make the same assumption.
t These determinations have little, if any, practical affect on our analysis if the commissioner is an
employee of the resort. He would then have an economic interest that would te analyzed under the same
materiality standard that applies to a business entity economic interest, or a source of income economic
interest that is a business entity, so we are merely avoiding applying the same analysis over again.


File No. I-09-076
Page No. 9
the business assuming he has made $500 or more within the l2 months preceding the
governmental decision. Additionally, if he owns more than l0 percent of tn" business, he
has an economic interest in any customers of the business if his pro rata share of the
income the resort receives from the customer equals $500 or more in the l2-month period
before the governmental decision.
Kevin Peterson: Commissioner Peterson owns a fishing guide business in which
he takes members of the public on fishing expeditions throughoui the state (including in
Mono County waters) and to locations outside the country such as Central America. He
too has an economic interest in the business entity as an employee of the business (and if
he has an investment interest of $2,000 or more) as well as a source of income economic
interest in the business assuming he has made $500 or more within the l2 months
preceding the governmental decision. Additionally, if he owns more than ten percent of
the business, he has an economic interest in any customers of the business if his pro rata
'share of the income the resort receives from thi customer equals $500 or more in the 12-
month period before the governmental decision.
Additional Potential Economic lnterests
The information provided indicates that at least two of the commissioners have an
economic interest in r.eaf property.6 You have not provided any information conceming
real property economic interests nor have you p.orlded any facts that indicate any other
potential economic interests. Furthermore, because, we do not have any information
regarding any customer who may be a source of income to any particular commissioner
identified above, and by the nature of the govemmental decision it does not seem
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision addressed would financially affect
any individual customer or real property interest, our analysis is limited to tfre potertiut
affects of the governmental decisions on the business entiiies identified above. If you
believe that the decision, once formulated, could potentially affect such interests and
w.o{d like us to provide a more complete analysii, please ieel free to request further
advice when those facts become apparent.
SrBp 4: Is THr EcoNolttc INrrnBsr Drnrcrr,y lNvor,vBn rN THE GovBnNnTENTAL
Drcrsrox?
"In order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involvid in the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704(a).) If * e"onbmic inrerest is not directly
involved in a govemmental decision, it is deemed to be indirectly involved. (Regulation
18704(a)- For economic interests in sources of income, Regulation lg7o4.l detlrmines
the applicable material standard. (Regulation 1970a(aXl).i
u The defrnition of 'Jurisdiction" includes real property that is located within .,or not more than
two miles outside rhe boundaries of the jurisdiction." lS."iiorizo:s.)


File No. I-09-076
Page No. l0
Regulation 187 04.1 (a) states:
"(a) A person, incruding business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the officiai's ug"r"y.-A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the isiuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,'
Under the facts you have given, each of the business entities are indirectly
involved in the governmental decisions.
Srrp 5: M.lrpnr.lr,rry STANDARn
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation I 8700(a).)
For a business entity, or a source of income that is a business entity, that is
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision, the materiality standards are set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c). (See Regutations 18705.1(a)(2) and 18i05.3(b)(1).) These
standards vary depending upon the size of the business. you have noi pior'iA"d any
information about the financial size of the business involved, but for small businesses
(those with net income of less than $500,000 in the most recent fiscal year) the
materiality standards are set forth in Regularion lg705.l(c)(4) as follows:
"(4) If 
^the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( r )-(3),
the financial effect of a governmental decision onih" business enilty is ' "
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
. $ 20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $ 5,000 0i mo.e; o.,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $ 20,000 or more.,,


Srrp 6: RrlsoNlnr,y FonrsnEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable', if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).j Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner(1975) I Fppa Ops. l9g.)
You have not provided any specific information about the governmental decisions
contemplated. Each decision appears to encompass some aspect conceming the raising
and potential stocking of fish in lakes and steams in Mono Ctunty that couid have some
financial impact on the economic interests of the commissioners involved. Without
specific facts regarding the governmental decisions and the potential financial impacts on
those businesses, we are unable to determine if the r"urorubiy foreseeable financial affect
would be material.
Generally, given the facts presented, it appears unlikely that any decisions relating
to the stocking of fish would have a "reasonably foreseeable" material financial effect on
the businesses involved as set forth above, but it also seems possible that given the right
conditions and particular circumstances, one of the thresholds could be mJt. It is up to
the public official to make a reasonable good faith assessment of all the facts present at
the time of the decision to determine whether or not a particular conflict exists. If you
need further assistance on this matter, when the time arises please write for further advice
and include all factors relating to the governmental decision and its potential impact on
the economic interest of any particular commissioner.
srrps 7,q.No 8: Punr,rc GBNrnlr,r,y AND Lpcar,r,y Rrqumro plRrrcrparron
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally,'or
the "legally required participation" exception would be applicable hire. Aicordingly, we
have not provided an analysis involving these steps
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Counsel
By: w
Lenkeit
Senior

Page No. 1l

sections.question:
Does John Webb, Ray Robles, Jeremy Ross, Jim King, or Kevin Peterson have a
conflict of interest that would prevent them from participating in any of the following
activities?
I The members are: John Webb, Ray Robles, Jeremy Ross, Jim King, and Kevin Peterson.
I Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4l Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
1. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the stocking of
fish in Little Virginia Lake or the other Virginia Lakes (as to Commissioner Webb), in
the June Lakes (as to Jeremy Ross), in Rock Creek Lake or Rock Creek (as to Jim
KnighQ or in any of the lakes in Mono County (as to Kevin Peterson and Ray Robles).
2. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the stocking of
fish in lakes other than Little Virginia Lake or the othei Virginia Lakes (a-s to John
Webb), other than the June Lakes (as to Jeremy Ross), or other than Rock Creek Lake or
Rock Creek (as to Jim King).
3. Making recorlmendations to the board of supervisors regarding the
expenditure of county funds to enhance the amount of fish raised o, Con*ay Ranch.
4. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the
expenditure of county funds for the general management and op"raiio, oithe Conway
Ranch fish-rearing facility.
, 
5' Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding ways in which
the county could raise revenue (such as fundraisers, taies, grants, etc.; toinhance the fish
rearing and stocking in Mono Lake waters.
6. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding actions the
County could take to enhance the amount of fish raisedby DFG or privatl hatcheries and
stocked in Mono County waters.
7. Making recorlmendations to the board of supervisors regarding actions the
county could take to increase fishing related tourism in Mono county.
sections.conclusion:
Any of the commissioners would have a conflict of interest participating in any of
the governmental decisions enumerated above if the governmental decision *o-uta have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the his economic interests as
discussed below.
sections.facts:
In January 2004 yott requested informal written advice from the Commission
regarding potential conflicts of interest involving two appointed members of the Mono
County Fisheries Commission (the "Fisheries Commission") given their ownership of
fishing-related businesses adjacent to highly-visited fishing areas within Mono County
(the "County"). (Simorc Advice Letter, No. I-04-013.) You now seek further advice on
the same questions posed in 2004 in light of recent changes in facts and circumstances
that you believe may alter the prior analysis.


File No. l-09-076
Specifically, in our response to your prior request, we determined that neither of
the two commissioners, upon who's behalf the request was made, had a conflict of
interest in participating in activities of the Fisheries Commission because it was advisory
only and, as such, the two commissioners were not "public officials" within the meaning
of the Act.
However, since the time that advice was given, the Mono county Board of
Supervisors (the "Board") has allocated $25,000 of general fund money to the Fisheries
Commission for its expenditure in support of recreational fishing in Mono County. [n
other words, the Fisheries Commission has been given decision-making authority with
respect to the expenditure of $25,000. This one-time allocation resulted from a request
made by the Fish Commission to the Board at its 2008-2009 fiscal year budget hearing.
The Fisheries Commission's original request was for $40,000.
Additionally, since 2004, several new members have been appointed to the
Fisheries Commission, and John Frederickson - who was one of the subjects of the prior
request, has resigned.
BACKGROUND: Mono County owns and operates a fish-rearing facility iocated
at Conway Ranch. Mono County uses fish raised at Conway Ranch to supplement fish
stocking in Mono County waters otherwise carried out by the California Oepartment of
Fish and Game (DFG), private parties, and the County itself (the latter two using fish
purchased from private hatcheries).
The Fisheries Commission was created by a resolution of the Board for the
purpose of advising the Board with respect to Conway Ranch and fish rearing and
stocking within the County generally. The duties of the Fisheries Commission include
making recommendations regarding where fish raised on Conway Ranch should be
stocked, making recommendations regarding expenditure of County funds to purchase
fish raised at other facilities for stocking in Mono County, making.."o.n-.ndations
regarding methods for enhancing the amount of fish raised and stocked by private entities
and/or DFG in Mono County, and making recommendations regarding ttre entrancement
of fishing-related tourism in Mono County. Additionally, the Fisheriei Commission is
charged with drafting a fisheries management plan for the County, which has not yet
been completed.
The Fisheries Commission was created by Resolution of the Board of Supervisors
for the purpose of advising the board with respect to the Conway Ranch and fistt-rearing
and stocking within the County generally. The duties of the Fisheries Commission
includes: making recommendations regarding where fish raised on Conway Ranch should
be stocked; making recommendations regarding the expenditure of county funds to
purchase fish raised at other facilities for stocking in Mono County; making
recommendations regarding methods for enhancing the amount of fish raised and stocked
by private entities and/or DFG in Mono County; and making recommendations regarding
the enhancement of fishing-related tourism in Mono County. Additionally, the Fisheries
Commission is charged with drafting a fisheries management plan for the County, which
has not yet been completed.
The Fisheries Commission's recommendations to the Board are advisory only
although, in the four years since it was created, the Board has declined to follow the
Fisheries Commission's advice in one instance only - when it did not approve its request
that $40,000 be placed in the Fisheries Commission's budget to be allocated in support of
recreational fish enhancement. As mentioned above, the Board instead allocated
$25,000, which has made the Fisheries Commission a decision maker as to the
expenditure of those funds.
Flcrs PornNrr.lr,r,y CREATTNG A CoNrurcr
l. One of the members of the Fisheries Commission, John Webb, owns a small
resort located adjacent to Little Virginia Lake, a popular fishing destination in Mono
County. The resort consists of 19 individual cabins, a small store at which food, beer,
souvenirs, sundries, and fishing, hiking, and camping supplies are sold, a small
restaurant, and a boat rental operation. Many of the resort guests have come to the area
to fish, and the lake has historically been stocked with fish by both DFG and the County,
including fish raised by the County at Conway Ranch. The resort offers the only lodging,
food, and supplies within several miles of Virginia Lake and the several other lakes
within that watershed (the "other Virginia Lakes").
2. Another member, Jim King, owns Rock Creek Resort adjacent to Rock Creek
Lake and Rock Creek. The resort consists of 11 rental cabins, a general store, caf6, and
boat rentals on Rock Creek. The store carries fishing-related supplies such as tackle and
bait, a well as camping and other supplies. Many of the resort's customers have come to
the area to fish. While the lake and resort themselves are actually located in tnyo County
- Mono's neighbor to the south - Mono County does stock (and supports the stocking
by others of) Rock Creek Lake and Rock Creek, as the County receives revenue from ihe
campgrounds in the area, which are frequented by fishermen visiting the lake and creek.
The resort is I quarter-mile from the Mono County line.
3. A third membei, Jeremy Ross, is employed by a sporting goods shop in June
Lake, which is the commercial hub for visitors recreating at Gull, Silver, June, and Grant
Lakes (collectively "the June Lakes") in Mono County. Each of the June Lakes is a
popular fishing spot and is stocked with fish by the County and the DFG, including with
fish raised at Conway Ranch. The sporting goods shop primarily sells fishing bait and
tackle, as do other shops in June Lake.
4. A fourth member, Ray Robles, owns a for-profit fishing guide business in
which he takes members of the public on fishing trips within the County, primarily on
Bridgeport Reservoir.


File No. t-09-076
5. A fifth member, Kevin Peterson owns a fishing guide business in which he
takes members of the public on fishing expeditions throughout the state (including in
Mono County waters) and to locations outside the country such as Central America.
Mr. Peterson has the ability to and does guide his customers to any water within the
County in which the customers wish to fish.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused bytheir own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Section
87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otirerwise
using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision iriwhich the official
has a financial interest.
The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether
an official has a disqualifying conflict of interest. (Regulation 18700.) The geneial rule,
however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official mat<es-, participates
in making, or otherwise uses his or her official positionio influence a governmental
decision which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect o, ore or more of his
or her financial interests.
Srnps 1: Anr rHE MEMBERS oF TlrE FTsHERTES CoMMrssroN punr,rc oFFrcrALs?
Section 82048 states that "public official" means "every member, officer,
employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency.;' Regulation lg70l(a)
provides that for purposes of section 52048 defining public official,ihe following
definitions apply:
"(l) 'Member' shall include, but not be rimited to, salaried or unsalaried
members of committees, boards or commissions with decisionmaking
authortty. A committee, board or commission possesses decision -utirg
authority whenever:
(A) 
It may make a final govemmental decision;
(B) 
It may compel a governmental decision; or it may prevent a
governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive po*er to
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto that may not 6e
overridden; or
(c) 
It makes substantive recommendations that are, and over
an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without
significant amendment or modification by anothei public official
or govemmental agency." (Emphasis added.)
ln our previous letter (Simon Advice Letter, No. I-04-013), we advised that
"[s]ince the individuals are not public officials at this time, they do not have a conflict
of interest within the meaning of the Act. (Regulation 18700(bxl).) However, . . . if
there is a history or track record of "rubber stamping" an advisory body's
recommendations, the advisory body will be considered to have decision-making
authority. (See, e.g., Andriese Advice Letter, No. 4-03-016; Traverso Advice Letter,
No. I-01-124; Baird Advice Letter, No. 4-94-299; czachAdvice Letter, No. A-91-
5o3; woodbury Advice Letter, 4-90-665; Paley Advice Letter, A-90-5g3; Korade
Advice Letter, 4-89-715; Ball Advice Letrer, I-89-671.)"
"we have in the past advised new advisory bodies that they are in fact
solely advisory until a history of recommendations has been established.
(Ball, Traverso, supra.) once there is a history of a particular advisory
body's recommendations being routinely accepted without amendmenl or
modification, the body converts from a solely advisory function to one of
making, or participating in the making of a governmental decision and
must be incorporated into a conflict of interest code. (Traverso, supra.)
Since we have no track record concerning the commission, we are not able
to determine if its members should be included in the commission's
conflict of interest code. Even if, at this time, as you have concluded, the
commission functions solely as "advisory" as opposed to "making," or
"participating in making," govemmental decisions, we suggest that you
monitor the commission's recommendations and the board of supervisors'
actions with regard to these recommendations over the next several
months and amend the commission's conflict of interest code if required.
If this does become the case, and the commission members are included in
the conflict of interest code, you may then contact us again for advice on
any potential conflict of interest issues."
You have now indicated that the Fisheries Commission has been given decision-
making authority with respect to the expenditure of $25,000, which has been allocated to
it by the Board. Additionally, you have indicated that in the four years since it was
created, the Board has declined to follow the Fisheries Commission's advice in one
instance only - when it did not approve its request that $40,000 be placed in the Fisheries
Commission's budget to be allocated in support of recreational fistr-enhancement but
allocated the $25,000 instead. Given these facts, we believe that the Fisheries
Commission "makes substantive recommendations that are, and over an extended period
of time have been, regularly approved without significant amendment or modification by
another public official or govemmental agency." Accordingly, members of the Fisheries
Commission now meet the definition of "public official.'l
Srep2: Ans rHE Mrunrns oF THE FrsHrnms Coprurssrox M.lrlNG, PARTICTpATTNG
IN M.lxrxc, oR OTHTRwISE Ustxc TnBm Orrrcllr PosrrtoNs to INrlunNCE A
DnctsroN?
As public officials under the Act, the Fish Commission members may not make,
participate in making, or otherwise use their official positions to influence any decisions
that will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of their economic
interests. They will be called upon to consider certain actions, (see above questions).
Therefore, they will be making, participating in making, or otherwise usinjtheir official
positions to influence a governmental decision.
Srrp 3: Do rnn CoprurssroNERs Hlvr A PoTENTTALLv Drsguu,myrNc EcoNoMrc
Ixrnnrsr?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
section 87103 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a materill
'financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
member of his or her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic
interests, including:
o An economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a); Reg. 1g703.1(a)); or in which he or
she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any fosition of
managembnt. (Section 87103(d); Regulation 1g703.1(b).)
o An economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more. (Section 87r03(b); Regulation lg7o3.z.)
o An economic interest in any source of income, including promised income,
aggregating $500 or more within l2 months prior to theGcision. (Section 87103(c);
Regulation 18703.3.)
o An economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $420
or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87l03ie); neg-utalion
18703.4.)
o An economic interest in his or her personal finances, including those of his or her
immediate family -- this is the "personal financial effects" rule. 1Sectiong7l03;
Regulation 18703.5.)
Section 82005 defines "business entity" as "any organization or enterprise
operated for profit, including but not limited to a proprietoiship, partnership, fi..,
business trust, joint venture, syndicate, corporation or association.,,
Economic Interests of Commissioners ldentified:
From the information you have provided, each commissioner has the following
economic interests:
John Webb: Assuming Commissioner Webb's investment in the resort he owns
near Little Virginia Lake is valued at $2,000 or more, he has an economic interest in a
business entity.4 He also has an economic interest in the resort as a business entity if he
holds a position of management in operating the resort. Finally, he has a source of
income economic interest in the resort if his share of the gross receipts equal $500 or
more in the l2-month period before the governmental decision, and if he owns more than
ten percent of the business, he has an economic interest in the customers of the business
if his pro rata share of the income the resort receives from the customer equals $500 or
more in the l2-month period before the governmental decision.
Jim King: commissioner King's ownership of Rock creek Resort would
ordinarily give him an economic interest in a business entity because of his investment
interest. However, because an investment interest does not include a business entity that
does business, plans to do business or has done business within the last two years in the
jurisdiction, he does not have an economic interest in the resort because of his investment
interest. (See Section82034.) Likewise, because the resort is located outside the
jurisdiction and you have not provided any information indicating that the resort is doing
business within the jurisdiction, planning to do business within th-e jurisdiction, or has
done business within the jurisdiction within the last two years, Commissioner King does
not have an economic interest in the resort as a source of income to him.5 (See Section
82030.) Commissioner King will only have an economic interest in the resort if he is
employed by or has a position of management with the resort. (See Section 87103(d);
Regulation 18703. l(b).)
Jeremy Ross: Commissioner Ross is employed by a sporting goods shop in June
Lake, Mono County, near the June Lakes. He therefore has an economic interest in the
sporting good shop as a business entity and as a source of income to him assuming he has
made $500 or more within the 12 months preceding the govemmental decision.
Ra)'Robles: Commissioner Robles owns a for-profit fishing guide business in
which he takes members of the public on fishing trips within the Couity. He has an
economic interest in the business entity as an employee of the business iand if he has an
investment interest of $2,000 or more) as well as a source of income economic interest in
a For each of the commissioners listed below whom also have an ownership interest in a business
entity, for purposes of our analysis we will make the same assumption.
t These determinations have little, if any, practical affect on our analysis if the commissioner is an
employee of the resort. He would then have an economic interest that would te analyzed under the same
materiality standard that applies to a business entity economic interest, or a source of income economic
interest that is a business entity, so we are merely avoiding applying the same analysis over again.
the business assuming he has made $500 or more within the l2 months preceding the
governmental decision. Additionally, if he owns more than l0 percent of tn" business, he
has an economic interest in any customers of the business if his pro rata share of the
income the resort receives from the customer equals $500 or more in the l2-month period
before the governmental decision.
Kevin Peterson: Commissioner Peterson owns a fishing guide business in which
he takes members of the public on fishing expeditions throughoui the state (including in
Mono County waters) and to locations outside the country such as Central America. He
too has an economic interest in the business entity as an employee of the business (and if
he has an investment interest of $2,000 or more) as well as a source of income economic
interest in the business assuming he has made $500 or more within the l2 months
preceding the governmental decision. Additionally, if he owns more than ten percent of
the business, he has an economic interest in any customers of the business if his pro rata
'share of the income the resort receives from thi customer equals $500 or more in the 12-
month period before the governmental decision.
Additional Potential Economic lnterests
The information provided indicates that at least two of the commissioners have an
economic interest in r.eaf property.6 You have not provided any information conceming
real property economic interests nor have you p.orlded any facts that indicate any other
potential economic interests. Furthermore, because, we do not have any information
regarding any customer who may be a source of income to any particular commissioner
identified above, and by the nature of the govemmental decision it does not seem
reasonably foreseeable that the govemmental decision addressed would financially affect
any individual customer or real property interest, our analysis is limited to tfre potertiut
affects of the governmental decisions on the business entiiies identified above. If you
believe that the decision, once formulated, could potentially affect such interests and
w.o{d like us to provide a more complete analysii, please ieel free to request further
advice when those facts become apparent.
SrBp 4: Is THr EcoNolttc INrrnBsr Drnrcrr,y lNvor,vBn rN THE GovBnNnTENTAL
Drcrsrox?
"In order to determine if a govemmental decision's reasonably foreseeable
financial effect on a given economic interest is material, it must first be determined if the
official's economic interest is directly involved or indirectly involvid in the
governmental decision." (Regulation 18704(a).) If * e"onbmic inrerest is not directly
involved in a govemmental decision, it is deemed to be indirectly involved. (Regulation
18704(a)- For economic interests in sources of income, Regulation lg7o4.l detlrmines
the applicable material standard. (Regulation 1970a(aXl).i
u The defrnition of 'Jurisdiction" includes real property that is located within .,or not more than
two miles outside rhe boundaries of the jurisdiction." lS."iiorizo:s.)


Page No. l0
Regulation 187 04.1 (a) states:
"(a) A person, incruding business entities, sources of income, and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an official's
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by
filing an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding
conceming the decision before the official or the officiai's ug"r"y.-A
person is the subject of a proceeding if a decision involves the isiuance,
renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the subject person.,'
Under the facts you have given, each of the business entities are indirectly
involved in the governmental decisions.
Srrp 5: M.lrpnr.lr,rry STANDARn
A conflict of interest arises only when the reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of a governmental decision on a public official's economic interest is material.
(Regulation I 8700(a).)
For a business entity, or a source of income that is a business entity, that is
indirectly involved in the govemmental decision, the materiality standards are set forth in
Regulation 18705.1(c). (See Regutations 18705.1(a)(2) and 18i05.3(b)(1).) These
standards vary depending upon the size of the business. you have noi pior'iA"d any
information about the financial size of the business involved, but for small businesses
(those with net income of less than $500,000 in the most recent fiscal year) the
materiality standards are set forth in Regularion lg705.l(c)(4) as follows:
"(4) If 
^the business entity is not covered by subdivisions (c)( r )-(3),
the financial effect of a governmental decision onih" business enilty is ' "
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
. $ 20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity
incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $ 5,000 0i mo.e; o.,
"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $ 20,000 or more.,,


Srrp 6: RrlsoNlnr,y FonrsnEABLE
An effect upon economic interests is considered "reasonably foreseeable', if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).j Whether the
financial consequences of a governmental decision are substantially likely at the time the
decision is made depends on the facts surrounding the decision. A financial effect need
not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner(1975) I Fppa Ops. l9g.)
You have not provided any specific information about the governmental decisions
contemplated. Each decision appears to encompass some aspect conceming the raising
and potential stocking of fish in lakes and steams in Mono Ctunty that couid have some
financial impact on the economic interests of the commissioners involved. Without
specific facts regarding the governmental decisions and the potential financial impacts on
those businesses, we are unable to determine if the r"urorubiy foreseeable financial affect
would be material.
Generally, given the facts presented, it appears unlikely that any decisions relating
to the stocking of fish would have a "reasonably foreseeable" material financial effect on
the businesses involved as set forth above, but it also seems possible that given the right
conditions and particular circumstances, one of the thresholds could be mJt. It is up to
the public official to make a reasonable good faith assessment of all the facts present at
the time of the decision to determine whether or not a particular conflict exists. If you
need further assistance on this matter, when the time arises please write for further advice
and include all factors relating to the governmental decision and its potential impact on
the economic interest of any particular commissioner.
srrps 7,q.No 8: Punr,rc GBNrnlr,r,y AND Lpcar,r,y Rrqumro plRrrcrparron
You have not presented any facts indicating that either the "public generally,'or
the "legally required participation" exception would be applicable hire. Aicordingly, we
have not provided an analysis involving these steps

QUESTION: Does John Webb, Ray Robles, Jeremy Ross, Jim King, or Kevin Peterson have a
conflict of interest that would prevent them from participating in any of the following
activities?
I The members are: John Webb, Ray Robles, Jeremy Ross, Jim King, and Kevin Peterson.
I Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 831 l4l Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed).
1. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the stocking of
fish in Little Virginia Lake or the other Virginia Lakes (as to Commissioner Webb), in
the June Lakes (as to Jeremy Ross), in Rock Creek Lake or Rock Creek (as to Jim
KnighQ or in any of the lakes in Mono County (as to Kevin Peterson and Ray Robles).
2. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the stocking of
fish in lakes other than Little Virginia Lake or the othei Virginia Lakes (a-s to John
Webb), other than the June Lakes (as to Jeremy Ross), or other than Rock Creek Lake or
Rock Creek (as to Jim King).
3. Making recorlmendations to the board of supervisors regarding the
expenditure of county funds to enhance the amount of fish raised o, Con*ay Ranch.
4. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding the
expenditure of county funds for the general management and op"raiio, oithe Conway
Ranch fish-rearing facility.
, 
5' Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding ways in which
the county could raise revenue (such as fundraisers, taies, grants, etc.; toinhance the fish
rearing and stocking in Mono Lake waters.
6. Making recommendations to the board of supervisors regarding actions the
County could take to enhance the amount of fish raisedby DFG or privatl hatcheries and
stocked in Mono County waters.
7. Making recorlmendations to the board of supervisors regarding actions the
county could take to increase fishing related tourism in Mono county.

CONCLUSION: Any of the commissioners would have a conflict of interest participating in any of
the governmental decisions enumerated above if the governmental decision *o-uta have a
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any of the his economic interests as
discussed below.
